## **Volley Revolver: A Novel Matrix-Encoding Method for Privacy-Preserving Neural Networks (Inference)**

**Anonymous Author(s)** Affiliation Address email

## **Abstract**

In this work, we present a novel matrix-encoding method that is particularly convenient for neural networks to make predictions in a privacy-preserving manner using homomorphic encryption. Based on this encoding method, we implement a convolutional neural network for handwritten image classification over encryption. For two matrices _A_ and _B_ to perform homomorphic multiplication, the main idea behind it, in a simple version, is to encrypt matrix _A_ and the transpose of matrix _B_ into two ciphertexts respectively. With additional operations, the homomorphic matrix multiplication can be calculated over encrypted matrices efficiently. For the convolution operation, we in advance span each convolution kernel to a matrix space of the same size as the input image so as to generate several ciphertexts, each of which is later used together with the ciphertext encrypting input images for calculating some of the final convolution results. We accumulate all these intermediate results and thus complete the convolution operation.

In a public cloud with 40 vCPUs, our convolutional neural network implementation on the MNIST testing dataset takes _∼_ 287 seconds to compute ten likelihoods of 32 encrypted images of size 28 _×_ 28 simultaneously. The data owner only needs to upload one ciphertext ( _∼_ 19 _._ 8 MB) encrypting these 32 images to the public cloud.

## 18 **1 Introduction**

19 Machine learning applied in some specific domains such as health and finance should preserve privacy 20 while processing private or confidential data to make accurate predictions. In this study, we focus on 21 privacy-preserving neural network inference, which aims to outsource a well-trained inference model 22 to a cloud service in order to make predictions on private data. For this purpose, the data should 23 be encrypted first and then sent to the cloud service that should not be capable of having access to 24 the raw data. Compared to other cryptology technologies such as Secure Multi-Party Computation, 25 Homomorphic Encryption (HE) provides the most stringent security for this task.

26 Combining HE with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) inference has been receiving more 27 and more attention in recent years since Gilad-Bachrach et al. [6] proposed a framework called 28 Cryptonets . Cryptonets applies neural networks to make accurate inferences on encrypted data 29 with high throughput. Chanranne et al. [2] extended this work to deeper CNN using a different 30 underlying software library called HElib [7] and leveraged batch normalization and training process 31 to develop better quality polynomial approximations of the ReLU function for stability and accuracy. 32 Chou et al. [4] developed a pruning and quantization approach with other deep-learning optimization 33 techniques and presented a method for encrypted neural networks inference, Faster CryptoNets . 34 Brutzkus et al. [1] developed new encoding methods other than the one used in Cryptonets for 35 representing data and presented the Low-Latency CryptoNets ( LoLa ) solution. Jiang et al. [9]
Submitted to 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). Do not distribute.
- 36 proposed an efficient evaluation strategy for secure outsourced matrix multiplication with the help of 37 a novel matrix-encoding method.

- 38 **Contributions** In this study, our contributions are in three main parts:

- 39 1. We introduce a novel data-encoding method for matrix multiplications on encrypted matrices, 40 Volley Revolver , which can be used to multiply matrices of arbitrary shape efficiently.
- 41 2. We propose a feasible evaluation strategy for convolution operation, by devising an efficient 42 homomorphic algorithm to sum some intermediate results of convolution operations.

- 43 3. We develop some simulated operations on the packed ciphertext encrypting an image dataset 44 as if there were multiple virtual ciphertexts inhabiting it, which provides a compelling new 45 perspective of viewing the dataset as a three-dimensional structure.

## **2 Preliminaries**

- 47 Let “ _⊕_ ” and “ _⊗_ ” denote the component-wise addition and multiplication respectively between 48 ciphertexts encrypting matrices and the ciphertext ct _.P_ the encryption of a matrix _P_ . Let _I_ [[(] _i[m]_ ][ _j_[)] ] 49 represent the single pixel of the _j_ -th element in the _i_ -th row of the _m_ -th image from the dataset.

- 50 **Homomorphic Encryption** Homomorphic Encryption is one kind of encryption but has its char51 acteristic in that over an HE system operations on encrypted data generate ciphertexts encrypting 52 the right results of corresponding operations on plaintext without decrypting the data nor requiring 53 access to the secret key. Since Gentry [5] presented the first fully homomorphic encryption scheme, 54 tackling the over three decades problem, much progress has been made on an efficient data encoding 55 scheme for the application of machine learning to HE. Cheon et al. [3] constructed an HE scheme 56 (CKKS) that can deal with this technique problem efficiently, coming up with a new procedure 57 called rescaling for approximate arithmetic in order to manage the magnitude of plaintext. Their 58 open-source library, HEAAN , like other HE libraries also supports the Single Instruction Multiple Data 59 (aka SIMD) manner [11] to encrypt multiple values into a single ciphertext.

- 60 Given the security parameter, HEAAN outputs a secret key _sk_ , a public key _pk_ , and other public keys 61 used for operations such as rotation. For simplicity, we will ignore the rescale operation and 62 deem the following operations to deal with the magnitude of plaintext automatedly. HEAAN has the 63 following functions to support the HE scheme:
- 64 1. Enc _pk_ ( _m_ ): For the public key _pk_ and a message vector _m_ , HEAAN encrypts the message _m_ 65 into a ciphertext ct .
- 66 2. Dec _sk_ ( ct ): Using the secret key, this algorithm returns the message vector encrypted by the 67 ciphertext ct .
- 68 3. Add ( ct 1, ct 2): This operation returns a new ciphertext that encrypts the message Dec _sk_ ( ct 1) 69 _⊕_ Dec _sk_ ( ct 2).
- 70 4. Mul ( ct 1, ct 2): This procedure returns a new ciphertext that encrypts the message Dec _sk_ ( ct 1) 71 _⊗_ Dec _sk_ ( ct 2).

- 72 5. Rot ( ct , _l_ ): This procedure generates a ciphertext encrypting a new plaintext vector obtained 73 by rotating the the original message vector _m_ encrypted by ct to the left by _l_ positions.

[10] propose 77 an efficient database encoding method to encrypt this matrix into a single ciphertext in a row-by-row 78 manner. ## 88 **3 Technical details**

- 89 We introduce a novel matrix-encoding method called Volley Revolver , which is particularly 90 suitable for secure matrix multiplication. The basic idea is to place each semantically-complete 91 information (such as an example in a dataset) into the corresponding row of a matrix and encrypt 92 this matrix into a single ciphertext. When applying it to private neural networks, Volley Revolver 93 puts the whole weights of every neural node into the corresponding row of a matrix, organizes all the 94 nodes from the same layer into this matrix, and encrypts this matrix into a single ciphertext.

> 108 **Homomorphic Matrix Multiplication** We report an efficient evaluation algorithm for homomorphic 109 matrix multiplication. This algorithm uses a ciphertext ct _.R_ encrypting zeros or a given value such 110 as the weight bias of a fully-connected layer as an accumulator and an operation RowShifter to 111 perform a specific kind of row shifting on the encrypted matrix _B_[¯] . RowShifter pops up the first row 112 of _B_[¯] and appends another corresponding already existing row of _B_[¯] :

113 For two ciphertexts ct _.A_ and ct _.B_[¯] , the algorithm for homomorphic matrix multiplication has _p_ 114 iterations. For the _k_ -th iteration where 0 _≤ k < p_ there are the following four steps:

115 Step 1. This step uses RowShifter on ct _.B_[¯] to generate a new ciphertext ct _.B_[¯] 1 and then com116 putes the homomorphic multiplication between ciphertexts ct _.A_ and ct _.B_[¯] 1 to get the resulting 117 product ct _.AB_[¯] 1. When _k_ = 0, in this case RowShifter just return a copy of the ciphertext ct _.B_[¯] .

118 Step 2. In this step, the public cloud applies SumColVec on ct _.AB_[¯] 1 to collect the summation of 119 the data in each row of _AB_[¯] 1 for some intermediate results, and obtain the ciphertext ct _.D_ .

120 Step 3. This step designs a special matrix _F_ to generate a ciphertext ct _.F_ for filtering out the 121 redundancy element in _D_ by one multiplication Mul ( ct _.F_ , ct _.D_ ), resulting the ciphertext ct _.D_ 1.
122 Step 4. The ciphertext ct _.R_ is then used to accumulate the intermediate ciphertext ct _.D_ 1.

123 The algorithm will repeat Steps 1 to 4 for _p_ times and finally aggregates all the intermediate cipher124 texts, returning the ciphertext ct _.C_ . Algorithm 1 shows how to perform our homomorphic matrix 125 multiplication. Figure 1 describes a simple case for Algorithm 1 where _m_ = 2, _n_ = 4 and _p_ = 2.

126 The calculation process of this method, especially for the simple case where _m_ = _p_ , is intuitively 127 similar to a special kind of revolver that can fire multiple bullets at once (The first matrix _A_ is 128 settled still while the second matrix _B_ is revolved). That is why we term our encoding method 129 " Volley Revolver ". In the real-world cases where _m_ mod _p_ = 0, the operation RowShifter can 130 be reduced to only need one rotation RowShifter = Rot ( ct _, n_ ), which is much more efficient and 131 should thus be adopted whenever possible. Corresponding to the neural networks, we can set the 132 number of neural nodes for each fully-connected layer to be a power of two to achieve this goal.

- 142 **An impractical algorithm** Given a grayscale image _I_ of size _h × w_ and a kernel _K_ of size _k × k_ 143 with its bias _k_ 0 such that _h_ and _w_ are both greater than _k_ , based on the assumption that this image 144 can _happen_ to be encrypted into a ciphertext ct _.I_ with no more or less vacant slots, we present an 145 efficient algorithm to compute the convolution operation. We set the stride size to the usual default 146 value (1 _,_ 1) and adopt no padding technique in this algorithm.

- 147 Before the algorithm starts, the kernel _K_ should be called by an operation that we term 148 Kernelspanner to in advance generate _k_[2] ciphertexts for most cases where _h ≥_ 2 _· k −_ 1 and 149 _w ≥_ 2 _· k −_ 1, each of which encrypts a matrix _Pi_ for 1 _≤ i ≤ k_[2] , using a map to span the _k × k_ 150 kernel to a _h × w_ matrix space. For a simple example that _h_ = 4, _w_ = 4 and _k_ = 2, Kernelspanner

152 Our impractical homomorphic algorithm for convolution operation also needs a ciphertext ct _.R_ to 153 accumulate the intermediate ciphertexts, which should be initially encrypted by the kernel bias _k_ 0. 154 This algorithm requires _k × k_ iterations and the _i_ -th iteration consists of the following four steps for 155 1 _≤ i ≤ k_[2] :
156 Step 1. For ciphertexts ct _.I_ and ct _.Pi_ , this step computes their multiplication and returns the 157 ciphertext ct _.IPi_ = Mul ( ct _.I,_ ct _.Pi_ ).
158 Step 2. To aggregate the values of some blocks of size _k × k_ , this step applies the procedure 159 SumForConv on the ciphertext ct _.IPi_ , obtaining the ciphertext ct _.D_ .

160 Step 3. The public cloud generates a ciphertext encrypting a specially-designed matrix in order to 161 filter out the garbage data in ct _.D_ by one multiplication, obtaining a ciphertext ct _.D_[¯] .

> 162 Step 4. In this step, the homomorphic convolution-operation algorithm updates the accumulator 163 ciphertext ct _.R_ by homomorphically adding ct _.D_[¯] to it, namely ct _.R_ = Add ( ct _.R,_ ct _.D_[¯] ).

164 Note that Steps 1–3 in this algorithm can be computed in parallel with _k × k_ threads. We describe 165 how to compute homomorphic convolution operation in Algorithm 2 in detail. Figure 2 describes a 166 simple case for the algorithm where _h_ = 3, _w_ = 4 and _k_ = 3.

169 **Encoding Method for Convolution Operation** For simplicity, we assume that the dataset _X ∈_ 170 R _[m][×][f]_ can be encrypted into a single ciphertext ct _.X_ , _m_ is a power of two, all the images are 171 grayscale and have the size _h × w_ . Volley Revolver encodes the dataset as a matrix using the 172 database encoding method [10] and deals with any CNN layer with a single formation. In most cases, 173 _h × w < f_ , if this happened, zero columns could be used for padding. Volley Revolver extends 174 this database encoding method [10] with some additional operations to view the dataset matrix _X_ as 175 a three-dimensional structure.

176 Algorithm 2 is a feasible and efficient way to calculate the secure convolution operation in an HE 177 domain. However, its working-environment assumption that the size of an image is exactly the length 178 of the plaintext, which rarely happens, is too strict to make it a practical algorithm, leaving this 179 algorithm directly useless. In addition, Algorithm 2 can only deal with one image at a time due to 180 the assumption that a single ciphertext only encrypts only one image, which is too inefficient for 181 real-world applications.

182 To solve these problems, Volley Revolver performs some simulated operations on the ciphertext 183 ct _.X_ to treat the two-dimensional dataset as a three-dimensional structure. These simulated opera184 tions together could simulate the first continual space of the same size as an image of each row of the 185 matrix encrypted in a real ciphertext as a virtual ciphertext that can perform all the HE operations. 186 Moreover, the number of plaintext slots is usually set to a large number and hence a single ciphertext 187 could encrypt several images. For example, the ciphertext encrypting the dataset _X ∈_ R _[m][×][f]_ could

7

188 be used to simulate _m_ virtual ciphertexts vct _i_ for 1 _≤ i ≤ m_ , as shown below:

189 Similar to an HE ciphertext, a virtual ciphertext has virtual HE operations: vEnc , vDec , vAdd , 190 vMul , vRescale , vBootstrapping and vRot . Except for vRot , others can be all inherited from the 191 corresponding HE operations. The HE operations, Add , Mul , Rescale and Bootstrapping , result 192 in the same corresponding virtual operations: vAdd , vMul , vRescale and vBootstrapping . The 193 virtual rotation operation vRot is much different from other virtual operations: it needs two rotation 194 operations over the real ciphertext. We only need to simulate the rotation operation on these virtual 195 ciphertexts to complete the simulation. The virtual rotation operation vRot ( ct _, r_ ), to rotate all the 196 virtual ciphertexts dwelling in the real ciphertext ct to the left by _r_ positions, has the following 197 simulation result:

198 To bring all the pieces together, we can use Algorithm 2 to perform convolution operations for 199 several images in parallel based on the simulation virtual ciphertexts. The most efficient part of 200 these simulated operations is that a sequence of operations on a real ciphertext results in the same 201 corresponding operations on the multiple virtual ciphertexts, which would suffice the real-world 202 applications.

## 203 **4 Privacy-preserving CNN Inference**

204 **Limitations on applying CNN to HE** Homomorphic Encryption cannot directly compute func205 tions such as the ReLU activation function. We use Octave to generate a degree-three polynomial 206 by the least square method and just initialize all the activation layers with this polynomial, leaving 207 the training process to determine the coefficients of polynomials for every activation layer. Other 208 computation operations, such as matrix multiplication in the fully-connected layer and convolution 209 operation in the convolutional layer, can also be performed by the algorithms we proposed above. 210 **Neural Networks Architecture** We adopt the same CNN architecture as [9] but with some different 211 hyperparameters. Our encoding method Volley Revolver can be used to build convolutional neural 212 networks as deep as it needs. However, in this case, the computation time will therefore increase 213 and bootstrapping will have to be used to refresh the ciphertext, resulting in more time-consuming. 214 Table 1 gives a description of our neural networks architecture on the MNIST dataset.

218 **Database** We evaluate our implementation of the homomorphic CNN model on the MNIST dataset 219 to each time calculate ten likelihoods for 32 encrypted images of handwritten digits. The MNIST 220 database includes a training dataset of 60 000 images and a testing dataset of 10 000, each image of 221 which is of size 28 _×_ 28. For such an image, each pixel is represented by a 256-level grayscale and 222 each image depicts a digit from zero to nine and is labeled with it.

223 **Building a model in the clear** In order to build a homomorphic model, we follow the normal 224 approach for the machine-learning training in the clear — except that we replace the normal ReLU 225 function with a polynomial approximation: we (1) train our CNN model described in Table 1 with 226 the MNIST training dataset being normalized into domain [0 _,_ 1], and then we (2) implement the 227 well-trained resulting CNN model from step (1) using the HE library and HE programming.

228 For step (1) we adopt the highly customizable library keras with Tensorflow , which provides us 229 with a simple framework for defining our own model layers such as the activation layer to enact the 230 polynomial activation function. After many attempts to obtain a decent CNN model, we finally get a 231 CNN model that could reach a precision of 98 _._ 66% on the testing dataset. We store the weights of 232 this model into a CSV file for the future use. In step (2) we use the HE programming to implement 233 the CNN model, accessing its weights from the CSV file generated by step (1). We normalize the 234 MNIST training dataset by dividing each pixel by the floating-point constant 255.

235 **Classifying encrypted inputs** We implement our homomorphic CNN inference with the library 236 HEAAN by [3]. Note that before encrypting the testing dataset of images, we also normalize the 237 MNIST testing dataset by dividing each pixel by the floating-point constant 255, just like the normal 238 procedure on the training dataset in the clear.

239 **Parameters** _._ We follow the notation of [10] and set the HE scheme parameters for our implementment: 240 ∆= 2[45] and ∆ _c_ = 2[20] ; slots = 32768; logQ = 1200 and logN = 16 to achieve a security level 241 of 80 _−_ bits. (see [8, 9] for more details on these parameters).

241

242 **Result** _._ We evaluate the performance of our implementation on the MNIST testing dataset of 10 243 000 images. Since in this case Volley Revolver encoding method can only deal with 32 MNIST 244 images at one time, we thus partition the 10 000 MNIST testing images into 313 blocks with the 245 last block being padded zeros to make it full. We then test the homomorphic CNN inference on 246 these 313 ciphertexts and finally obtain a classification accuracy of 98 _._ 61%. The processing of each 247 ciphertext outputs 32 digits with the highest probability of each image, and it takes _∼_ 287 seconds on 248 a cloud server with 40 vCPUs. There is a slight difference in the accuracy between the clear and the 249 encryption, which is due to the fact that the accuracy under the ciphertext is not the same as that under 250 the plaintext. In order to save the modulus, a TensorFlow Lite model could be used to reduce the 251 accuracy in the clear from float 32 to float 16. progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper introduces "Volley Revolver," a matrix-encoding scheme designed to optimize privacy-preserving CNN inference via homomorphic encryption. While the premise is technically sound and addresses a relevant challenge in secure machine learning, the manuscript currently suffers from a lack of formal rigor and structural clarity. The description of the core encoding method and the convolution evaluation strategy is overly colloquial, relying on vague terminology ("in advance span each convolution kernel") that obscures the underlying mathematical transformation. Furthermore, the "Preliminaries" section is somewhat uneven; it spends significant space explaining basic properties of Homomorphic Encryption that are standard in the field, yet it provides confusing or poorly formatted notation for the proposed `SumForConv` procedure, which is the paper's primary contribution. To achieve the standard required for a venue like NeurIPS, the authors must shift from a descriptive narrative to a precise algorithmic presentation, explicitly defining the space complexity of the "three-dimensional structure" mentioned and providing a more rigorous comparison against existing work (e.g., LoLa or Faster CryptoNets) to substantiate the claimed efficiency gains beyond mere wall-clock time.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript proposes "Volley Revolver," a matrix-encoding method for privacy-preserving neural network inference using homomorphic encryption, but the work currently exhibits significant weaknesses in establishing novelty, technical precision, and evaluative context. The core mechanism described—encrypting matrix $A$ and the transpose of $B$ to enable homomorphic multiplication—appears to align closely with standard techniques employed in existing CKKS-based frameworks like Cryptonets and LoLa, yet the paper fails to clearly articulate how this encoding differs from or improves upon these established baselines. Clarity is further hindered by the use of vague, non-standard terminology such as "virtual ciphertexts" and "simulated operations," which obscures the actual contribution regarding slot packing and permutation and should be replaced with rigorous HE-specific definitions. Most critically, the experimental evaluation is underdeveloped; the reported latency of $\sim 287$ seconds for 32 images is presented without comparison to state-of-the-art methods, rendering the performance claims meaningless. Additionally, the preliminary section's approach to rescaling operations—stating they can be "ignored" and "deemed automated"—is a major technical oversight, as precision management and noise growth are fundamental challenges in approximate HE schemes like CKKS that must be addressed with quantitative analysis rather than abstraction.

comments.title: Dimensional misalignment in filtering step (Step 3)
comments.quote: Step 3. This step designs a special matrix _F_ to generate a ciphertext ct _.F_ for filtering out the redundancy element in _D_ by one multiplication Mul ( ct _.F_ , ct _.D_ ), resulting the ciphertext ct _.D_ 1.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the component-wise multiplication between `ct_.F` and `ct_.D`. Checking Algorithm 1, I noted that `F` is explicitly defined as $m \times n$, while `ct_.D` is the output of `SumColVec` applied to an $m \times n$ matrix. In HEAAN/CKKS, summing an $m \times n$ packed matrix along one dimension yields a plaintext vector of length $m$. Homomorphic component-wise multiplication (`Mul`) strictly requires both operands to occupy the exact same number of slots with aligned structure. I checked the surrounding text for any mention of padding, broadcasting, or slot expansion, but found none. What remains problematic is that the passage does not specify how the $m$-dimensional vector `ct_.D` is reshaped or aligned to match the $m \times n$ structure of `ct_.F` before multiplication. Without this detail, the filtering operation is technically underspecified and could lead to slot misalignment or incorrect results in implementation.

I considered whether `SumColVec` might be a library-specific convention for row aggregation, but in Section 2 the authors separately define both `SumRowVec` and `SumColVec`, implying they are distinct primitives. comments.quote: For simplicity, we assume that each of the three matrices _A_ , _B_ and _C_ could be encrypted into a single ciphertext.
comments.explanation: The statement that matrices $A$, $B$, and $C$ can each be encrypted into a single ciphertext caught my attention as a strong practical assumption. I checked the Preliminaries and Abstract, where the authors reference fixed slot capacities ($N$) and specific data sizes for MNIST inference. I recognized that this 'simplicity' disclaimer glosses over the fundamental batch-processing constraints of CKKS/HEAAN. While acceptable for a high-level theoretical sketch, it masks the reality that homomorphic matrix multiplication often requires chunking, tiling, or multi-ciphertext strategies when $m \times n$ or $m \times p$ approaches or exceeds $N$. I acknowledged that the authors explicitly label it 'for simplicity', but given the paper's focus on practical inference efficiency and cloud deployment, this assumption is presented without bounds. comments.title: Dimensional misalignment in filtering step (Step 3)
comments.quote: Step 3. This step designs a special matrix _F_ to generate a ciphertext ct _.F_ for filtering out the redundancy element in _D_ by one multiplication Mul ( ct _.F_ , ct _.D_ ), resulting the ciphertext ct _.D_ 1.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the component-wise multiplication between `ct_.F` and `ct_.D`. Checking Algorithm 1, I noted that `F` is explicitly defined as $m 	imes n$, while `ct_.D` is the output of `SumColVec` applied to an $m 	imes n$ matrix. In HEAAN/CKKS, summing an $m 	imes n$ packed matrix along one dimension yields a plaintext vector of length $m$. Homomorphic component-wise multiplication (`Mul`) strictly requires both operands to occupy the exact same number of slots with aligned structure. I checked the surrounding text for any mention of padding, broadcasting, or slot expansion, but found none. What remains problematic is that the passage does not specify how the $m$-dimensional vector `ct_.D` is reshaped or aligned to match the $m 	imes n$ structure of `ct_.F` before multiplication. Without this detail, the filtering operation is technically underspecified and could lead to slot misalignment or incorrect results in implementation.

comments.title: Misleading claim regarding RowShifter efficiency reduction
comments.quote: In the real-world cases where _m_ mod _p_ = 0, the operation RowShifter can be reduced to only need one rotation RowShifter = Rot ( ct _, n_ ), which is much more efficient and should thus be adopted whenever possible.
comments.explanation: The claim that `RowShifter` reduces to 'only need one rotation' initially struck me as contradictory to the algorithm's $p$-iteration loop. I checked the context to understand their intent: when $m$ is a multiple of $p$, the periodic repetition in the padded transpose $B^	op$ aligns perfectly with the rotation cycle, eliminating the need for complex index-based masking or partial shifts. However, the phrasing implies a reduction in the total number of shift operations. In HE, rotating by $n$ slots shifts rows down by one position; to compute all $p$ columns of the product matrix $C$, the algorithm must still iterate $p$ times, requiring $p$ distinct alignments (or $p$ rotations). I verified that even with perfect periodicity, the computational steps are not reduced to a single rotation. What remains problematic is the ambiguity: a careful reader could incorrectly infer that the outer loop is eliminated or that the asymptotic complexity drops to $O(1)$ rotations, which misrepresents the algorithm's actual flow and efficiency gains.

comments.title: Counter-intuitive naming vs. description for SumColVec
comments.quote: In this step, the public cloud applies SumColVec on ct _.AB_[¯] 1 to collect the summation of the data in each row of _AB_[¯] 1 for some intermediate results, and obtain the ciphertext ct _.D_ .
comments.explanation: I initially confused the function name `SumColVec` with its described behavior. The name suggests summing columns, but the authors explicitly state it collects 'the summation of the data in each row'. I checked standard HE literature and convolution primitives: summing across columns typically yields a vector of column sums, while summing across rows yields row sums. I considered whether `SumColVec` might be a library-specific convention for row aggregation, but in Section 2 the authors separately define both `SumRowVec` and `SumColVec`, implying they are distinct primitives. I checked the mathematical context: to compute row sums for the matrix product, one must sum elements along the column index for each row. This matches the described behavior, but contradicts the function name. What remains problematic is the direct inversion between the primitive's name and its mathematical effect, which could easily mislead implementers into selecting the wrong operation or misunderstanding the data flow in their own HE code.

comments.title: Oversimplified ciphertext capacity assumption
comments.quote: For simplicity, we assume that each of the three matrices _A_ , _B_ and _C_ could be encrypted into a single ciphertext.
comments.explanation: The statement that matrices $A$, $B$, and $C$ can each be encrypted into a single ciphertext caught my attention as a strong practical assumption. I checked the Preliminaries and Abstract, where the authors reference fixed slot capacities ($N$) and specific data sizes for MNIST inference. I recognized that this 'simplicity' disclaimer glosses over the fundamental batch-processing constraints of CKKS/HEAAN. While acceptable for a high-level theoretical sketch, it masks the reality that homomorphic matrix multiplication often requires chunking, tiling, or multi-ciphertext strategies when $m 	imes n$ or $m 	imes p$ approaches or exceeds $N$. I acknowledged that the authors explicitly label it 'for simplicity', but given the paper's focus on practical inference efficiency and cloud deployment, this assumption is presented without bounds. What remains problematic is that it could mislead readers about actual memory overhead and hardware requirements, making it difficult to assess the method's scalability to larger neural network layers without additional assumptions or tiling strategies.

comments.title: Contradictory filtering condition in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: if ( _wth − i_ ) mod _k_ = 0 **and** _wth_ + _k ≤ w_ **and** ( _hth − j_ ) mod _k_ = 0 **and** _hth_ + _k ≤ h_ **then**
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state in Section 3.2 that the convolution uses a 'stride size to the usual default value (1, 1)'. However, the filtering condition in Algorithm 2 lines 16-17 uses `(wth - i) mod k = 0`. This modulo condition only preserves output coordinates that are spaced `k` positions apart, which corresponds to a stride of `k` (or block-wise processing), not stride 1. For a standard stride-1 convolution, this condition would incorrectly discard valid adjacent output pixels (e.g., with k=3, i=0, it keeps wth=0, 3, 6... but drops wth=1, 2, 4, 5, which are necessary for the sliding window). This logical error undermines the correctness and reproducibility of the convolution procedure as described.

comments.title: RowShifter parameter and definition mismatch
comments.quote:
RowShifter(ct, n) = Rot(ct, n) when m mod p = 0
ct _T ←_ RowShifter ( ct _B,_ p, idx_ )
comments.explanation: The text defines `RowShifter` as a row-rotation operation equivalent to `Rot(ct, n)` (shifting by `n` slots, corresponding to one row), and notes a simplification when `m mod p = 0`. However, Algorithm 1 calls `RowShifter(ct.B, p, idx)` with three arguments that are never defined in the accompanying prose. It is unclear whether `p` is a fixed shift step, `idx` is an iteration counter, or how they compose to produce the total rotation across the `p` loop iterations. Without clarifying the mapping between `RowShifter` and the underlying `Rot` primitive, and how the `idx` parameter accumulates shifts, the algorithm is underspecified for implementation.

comments.title: Inconsistent matrix indexing in Figure 1
comments.quote: Encoding a 0 a 1 a 2 a 3 × b 0 b 2 b 4 b 6
comments.explanation: Figure 1 illustrates the encoding with `p=2, n=4, m=2`. According to the `τ_b` encoding map defined in Section 3.1, matrix `B` (size `n×p = 4×2`) is transposed and vertically extended to form `B_bar` (size `m×n = 2×4`). The first row of `B_bar` should contain the first column of `B` repeated: `b_{1,1}, b_{2,1}, b_{3,1}, b_{4,1}`. However, Figure 1 shows the first row as `b0 b2 b4 b6`, which implies a row-major flattening or an alternate indexing convention that contradicts the column-wise vertical extension described in the text. This notation inconsistency makes it difficult to trace how the homomorphic multiplication and `SumColVec` aggregation in the figure correspond to the formal definition, though the overall 'revolver' concept remains clear.

comments.title: Incorrect dimension comment in Algorithm 2
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2 line 13, the comment states `F ∈ R_{m×n}`. This appears to be a copy-paste error from Algorithm 1, which operates on matrices of size `m×n`. In the context of Algorithm 2 (convolution), `F` is a binary filtering matrix applied to the image `I` of size `h×w`, so it should be of dimensions `h×w`. While this is a minor typographical issue, it introduces unnecessary confusion regarding the dimensions of the filter matrix and could mislead a careful reader attempting to reproduce the filtering step.

comments.title: OCR corruption obscures SumForConv definition
comments.quote:
Below we illustrate the result of SumForConv on _Z_ taking the example that _n_ and _f_ are both 4 and the kernel size is 3 _×_ 3:

    [section 0] 86 where _s_ [ _i_ ][ _j_ ] =[] _[i] p_[+2] = _i_  _jq_ +2= _j[z]_[[] _[p]_[][] _[q]_[]][ for][ 1] _[ ≤][i, j][≤]_[2][. In the convolutional layer,][ SumForConv][ can help] 87 to compute some partial results of convolution operation for an image simultaneously.
comments.explanation: The authors intend to illustrate how SumForConv aggregates k×k blocks to compute partial convolution results. However, the mathematical illustration or formula following this sentence is completely garbled by OCR, making the procedure's exact mechanics irrecoverable from the text. Without the explicit mapping or summation logic, the method remains underspecified for reproduction.

comments.title: Missing formula for virtual rotation vRot
comments.quote: The virtual rotation operation vRot ( ct _, r_ ), to rotate all the virtual ciphertexts dwelling in the real ciphertext ct to the left by _r_ positions, has the following simulation result:
comments.explanation: The text introduces the need for vRot and explicitly states it simulates rotation on virtual ciphertexts, claiming it 'needs two rotation operations over the real ciphertext'. However, the sentence ends with a colon expecting a formula or algorithm, which is missing (likely an OCR skip of a figure/equation). This leaves the critical simulation step underspecified.

comments.title: Incorrect matrix dimension comment in Algorithm 2
comments.explanation: The comment claims the filtering mask `F` has dimensions $m \times n$, which were defined in Section 3.1 for the matrix multiplication task. However, Algorithm 2 operates on images of size $h \times w$, and the subsequent loops for populating `F` iterate `hth` from `0` to `h-1` and `wth` from `0` to `w-1`. Thus, `F` is actually $h \times w$. This is a clear copy-paste error from the earlier section that could mislead readers about the mask's shape.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameters in RowShifter call
comments.quote: ct_.T ← RowShifter ( ct_.B, p, idx_[¯] )
comments.explanation: The summary defines `RowShifter(ct, r, idx)`, but Algorithm 1 line 4 passes `p` (the number of columns in B) as the second argument. In homomorphic matrix multiplication using packed ciphertexts, cyclically shifting rows typically requires a shift amount proportional to the row width (e.g., `n` positions). Passing `p` here is confusing and likely a typo for `n`, or indicates an unclear parameter-passing convention. The relationship between `p`, `idx`, and the actual rotation amount is not resolved by the surrounding text.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of CONV layer inputs
comments.quote: CONV|32 input images of size28_×_28,4 kernels of size3_×_3,stride size of(1,1)|
comments.explanation: Standard CNN terminology describes layers by input channels, not 'input images'. The authors likely mean 32 *virtual* images packed into a single ciphertext (as detailed in the virtual ciphertext section), but phrasing it as '32 input images' in the architecture table is misleading. A careful reader might initially confuse this with channel multiplicity rather than ciphertext packing.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: Based on the initial portion of the paper, the manuscript addresses a relevant and timely topic—privacy-preserving neural network inference using homomorphic encryption—and proposes a novel encoding method called Volley Revolver, along with a convolution evaluation strategy. However, the clarity and presentation quality are significantly hindered by numerous typographical errors, inconsistent notation, and missing details that undermine the paper’s readability and credibility. The abstract and introduction are dense and lack clear motivation or intuition for the proposed method, making it difficult to assess the novelty relative to existing works (e.g., CryptoNets, LoLa). The most critical issue is that the core contribution—the Volley Revolver encoding—is only vaguely described; the reader is left without a concrete explanation of how the encoding works, how it differs from prior matrix-packing approaches, or why it is particularly efficient. Furthermore, the preliminaries section contains garbled syntax (e.g., “I[[(]i[m]][j[)]]”), missing citations for cited works (only numeric references are given), and incomplete definitions of key procedures (SumRowVec, SumColVec, SumForConv) that are central to the later exposition. Without a clear, well-structured description of the encoding method and its advantages early on, the paper risks appearing as a collection of heuristics rather than a principled contribution. Before resubmission, the authors should thoroughly proofread the text, provide a self-contained definition of Volley Revolver, and offer a comparative analysis to justify its claimed efficiency.

comments.title: Typo in author name 'Chanranne'
comments.quote: Chanranne et al. [2] extended this work to deeper CNN using a different underlying software library called HElib [7] and leveraged batch normalization and training process
comments.explanation: The name 'Chanranne' appears to be a typo. The correct name should likely be 'Chabanne' (Herve Chabanne et al., 2017, 'Privately Evaluating Decision Trees and Random Forests' or similar works in privacy-preserving ML). Given the context of extending CryptoNets with HElib and polynomial approximations of ReLU, this likely refers to Chabanne et al. However, since this is a citation that could be resolved by checking the bibliography, and the paper body is not available for verification, I will flag it as a minor issue.

comments.title: Repeated word 'the the'
comments.quote: by rotating the the original message vector _m_ encrypted by ct to the left by _l_ positions.
comments.explanation: There is a duplicated definite article 'the the'. This is a grammatical error that does not affect technical understanding but indicates sloppiness. Standard editing would remove the duplicate.

comments.title: Missing matrix illustrations for critical operations (SumRowVec, SumColVec, SumForConv, incomplete column shifting)
comments.quote: The matrix obtained from matrix _Z_ by the _incomplete_ column shifting operation is shown as follows: 2
comments.explanation: Multiple promised matrix illustrations are missing (replaced by stray numbers or end-of-section markers). These include: (1) the result of 'incomplete column shifting' in paragraph 18, (2) the results of SumRowVec and SumColVec in paragraph 19, and (3) the result of SumForConv in paragraph 20. These illustrations are essential for understanding the key operations that form the basis of the algorithms. Their absence significantly harms reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified RowShifter operation and inconsistent parameter usage
comments.quote: RowShifter pops up the first row of _B_¯ and appends another corresponding already existing row of _B_¯
comments.explanation: The RowShifter operation is described vaguely ('pops up the first row and appends another already existing row') and the visual depiction suggests a cyclic shift, but this is never explicitly stated. Furthermore, Algorithm 1 calls RowShifter with three parameters (ct.B, p, idx) while the prose description only shows it operating on a single ciphertext. The role of parameters p and idx is not explained, making the algorithm's behavior underspecified and non-reproducible.

comments.title: Corrupted formula defining SumForConv result
comments.explanation: The formula defining s[i][j] is garbled with unreadable characters and malformed LaTeX, likely due to OCR corruption. While a careful reader might infer the intended meaning (block sum over a 3×3 kernel), the text as presented is not interpretable as mathematics.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of virtual ciphertexts and simulated operations
comments.quote: Volley Revolver performs some simulated operations on the ciphertext ct.X to treat the two-dimensional dataset as a three-dimensional structure. These simulated operations together could simulate the first continual space of the same size as an image of each row of the matrix encrypted in a real ciphertext as a virtual ciphertext that can perform all the HE operations.
comments.explanation: This paragraph is very vague. The concepts of 'simulated operations' and 'virtual ciphertext' are not clearly defined, and the phrase 'first continual space of the same size as an image of each row' is grammatically awkward and unclear. This underspecification makes it impossible for a reader to understand or reproduce the claimed contribution.

comments.title: Potential index error in filter matrix construction (Algorithm 1, line 9)
comments.quote: F[i][(i+idx)%n] ← 1
comments.explanation: The filter matrix F is constructed with dimensions m×n. The loop sets F[i][(i+idx)%n] = 1 for i from 1 to m. However, if m > n, the index i may not correspond to a valid row index for the intermediate matrix D (which has m rows after SumColVec). The purpose of this filter is to 'clean up the redundancy' — it seems to select one element per row. But if m ≠ n, the pattern F[i][(i+idx)%n] may not correctly align with the positions where meaningful sum values reside. The authors claim that cases where m mod p = 0 allow optimization, but the general case remains unclear.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 uses inconsistent dimensions (m,n instead of h,w) and missing SumForConv implementation
comments.quote: Set F ← 0 ▷F ∈ R[m][×][n]
comments.explanation: Two issues compound here: (1) The filter matrix F in Algorithm 2 is declared with dimensions m×n, but the convolution algorithm uses image dimensions h and w. This appears to be a copy-paste error from Algorithm 1 (matrix multiplication context). The loops use hth and wth, confirming the dimensions should be h×w. (2) Algorithm 2 calls SumForConv (line 12) but provides no formal definition or implementation of this subroutine. The paper only illustrates its result with a 4×4 example, not a general method. This significantly hinders reproducibility.

comments.explanation: The promised illustration of SumForConv is missing, replaced only by the end of section marker. This operation is central to the convolution algorithm (Algorithm 2), and its lack of explicit definition makes the subsequent description of the convolution algorithm underspecified.

comments.title: Underspecified RowShifter operation in matrix multiplication algorithm
comments.quote: RowShifter pops up the first row of _B_¯ and appends another corresponding already existing row of _B_¯
comments.explanation: The prose describes RowShifter removing the first row and appending 'another corresponding already existing row', but the matrix illustration below shows a more complex operation: it appears to cyclically shift each column of _B_¯ upward (or equivalently, rotate each column's elements). The description is ambiguous about what 'another corresponding already existing row' means—is it the row that was originally second, or a row from a different position? The parameter _r_ in the illustration seems to represent _(m-1)%p_ from the earlier encoding, but this is not explained. Algorithm 1 calls RowShifter with parameters (ct.B, p, idx), but the function signature is not formally defined, making it unclear how _idx_ controls the shift amount.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 references undefined function RowShifter signature
comments.quote: ct.T ← RowShifter ( ct.B, p, idx_¯ )
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 calls RowShifter with three arguments (ciphertext, p, idx), but the earlier prose description and illustration of RowShifter only showed it taking one argument (the ciphertext of _B_¯). The role of parameters _p_ and _idx_ is not explained. From context, _idx_ likely controls which of the _p_ iterations we are in, and _p_ may be the number of columns of the original _B_. However, the underscore after 'idx' (written as 'idx_ ') appears to be an OCR artifact (perhaps a subscript), adding confusion. The algorithm is not reproducible without clarifying how RowShifter uses these parameters.

comments.title: Potential index error in filter matrix construction (Algorithm 1, line 9)
comments.quote: F[i][(i+idx)%n] ← 1
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the filter matrix _F_ is constructed with dimensions _m×n_ (as stated in the comment). However, the loop iterates _i_ from 1 to _m_, and sets _F[i][(i+idx)%n]_ to 1. Since _n_ is the number of columns of _A_ (which is also the number of rows of _B_ and the number of columns of _B_¯ after encoding), and _F_ is _m×n_, using _n_ in the modulo makes sense. However, the access pattern _F[i][(i+idx)%n]_ when _i_ goes to _m_ may be problematic if _m > n_ because the index _i_ might not correspond to a valid row index for the intermediate matrix _D_ (which supposedly has _m_ rows after SumColVec). The purpose of this filter is to 'clean up the redundancy' — it seems to select one element per row. But if _m ≠ n_, the pattern _F[i][(i+idx)%n]_ may not correctly align with the positions where meaningful sum values reside. The authors claim later that cases where _m mod p = 0_ allow optimization, but the general case remains unclear.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 constructs filter matrix _F_ with dimensions _m×n_ but _m_ and _n_ are not defined in that context
comments.quote: Set F ← 0 ▷F ∈ R[m][×][n]
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the filter matrix _F_ is declared with dimensions _m×n_, but _m_ and _n_ are not defined in the algorithm's context or the surrounding text. Earlier in Section 3.1, _m_ and _n_ were used for matrix multiplication dimensions, but in Section 3.2, the variables for image dimensions are _h_ and _w_. This appears to be a copy-paste error from Algorithm 1. The correct dimension should likely be _h×w_. The loops that follow use _hth_ from 0 to _h-1_ and _wth_ from 0 to _w-1_, which confirms the dimension should be _h×w_.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 filter matrix condition may have off-by-one errors
comments.quote: if (wth − i) mod k = 0 and wth + k ≤ w and (hth − j) mod k = 0 and hth + k ≤ h then
comments.explanation: The conditions are meant to select positions where convolution results are valid. However, the indices _i_ and _j_ in Algorithm 2 loop from 0 to _k-1_ (lines 9-10), corresponding to kernel row and column offsets. For stride 1 convolution with no padding, valid output positions are those where the kernel fits entirely within the image: _hth_ ∈ [0, h-k] and _wth_ ∈ [0, w-k]. The condition _wth + k ≤ w_ is correct, but the modulo condition _(wth − i) mod k = 0_ with _i_ fixed seems to select only those positions where _wth ≡ i (mod k)_. This would only cover 1/k of the columns for each iteration, which is intentional (each kernel element's contribution is handled separately). However, the condition _wth + k ≤ w_ combined with _wth ≡ i (mod k)_ means that for some iteration _i_, the last valid column index _(w-k)_ might not satisfy the congruence, potentially missing the last column's contribution. For example, if _w=5_, _k=3_, then valid output columns are 0,1,2. For _i=2_, the condition selects _wth_ ≡ 2 mod 3, so _wth=2_ is valid and 2+3=5 ≤ 5 holds. But for _i=0_, _wth=0,3_; 3+3=6 > 5 so only _wth=0_ is selected. This seems correct. The concern is whether the boundary condition should be _wth + k < w_ or _wth + k ≤ w_ — in standard C-style indexing with 0-indexed positions, the last valid starting position is _w-k_, and _wth + k - 1_ is the last pixel included. The condition _wth + k ≤ w_ means _wth ≤ w-k_, which is correct for 0-indexed _wth_ (since _wth_ starts at 0, the max valid is _w-k_). So this is actually correct. No error here after careful analysis.

comments.title: Figure 1 text describes multiplication but uses inconsistent notation for matrix entries
comments.explanation: The figure text uses 0-indexed variables like _a0, a1, ... b0, b1, ..._, but the paper's notation in Section 3.1 uses 1-indexed notation _a[1][1], b[1][1]_, etc. This inconsistency between figure and text could confuse a careful reader trying to map the example to the formal description. However, figure captions often use simplified notation for clarity. This is a minor inconsistency.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of virtual ciphertexts
comments.quote: Volley Revolver performs some simulated operations on the ciphertext ct.X to treat the two-dimensional dataset as a three-dimensional structure. These simulated operations together could simulate the first continual space of the same size as an image of each row of the matrix encrypted in a real ciphertext as a virtual ciphertext that can perform all the HE operations.
comments.explanation: This paragraph is very vague. What are 'simulated operations'? How does one simulate a 'virtual ciphertext'? The concept of treating a 2D dataset as 3D is introduced as a key contribution (point 3), but the description here is insufficient for a reader to understand how it works. The phrase 'first continual space of the same size as an image of each row' is grammatically awkward and unclear. This underspecification makes it impossible for a reader to reproduce the method.

comments.title: Corrupted formula for SumForConv result
comments.explanation: The formula defining s[i][j] is garbled, containing unreadable characters (e.g., [], ) and malformed LaTeX. It appears to be an OCR corruption of an intended double summation: s[i][j] = sum_{p=i}^{i+2} sum_{q=j}^{j+2} z[p][q] for 1 ≤ i,j ≤ 2. The context prior to the passage (line 85) states that n and f are both 4 and kernel size is 3×3, so the intended formula is a straightforward 3×3 block sum. A careful reader can guess the intended meaning, but the text as presented is not interpretable as mathematics.

comments.title: RowShifter in Algorithm 1 called with unmatched parameters
comments.quote: ct _.T ←_ RowShifter ( ct _.B, p, idx_[¯] )
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 (lines 4), RowShifter is called with arguments (ct_.B, p, idx_). However, the textual description of RowShifter earlier (lines 111-112) defines it as operating on a matrix (or its encryption) by popping the first row and appending another row; it does not specify parameters p and idx. The figure (line 130) later suggests RowShifter can be reduced to Rot(ct, n) when m mod p = 0, which is a single rotation by n positions. The meaning of 'p' and 'idx' in the algorithm call is not defined or explained, and the algorithm does not specify how the shift amount depends on the iteration idx. This makes the algorithm underspecified: a reader cannot determine what rotation or row-permutation is actually applied at each iteration.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of RowShifter in matrix multiplication section
comments.quote: RowShifter pops up the first row of _B_[¯] and appends another corresponding already existing row of _B_[¯]
comments.explanation: The description of RowShifter is vague. It says it 'pops up the first row and appends another already existing row', but it is not clear which row is appended, nor how the operation relates to the 'p' and 'idx' parameters in Algorithm 1. The visual depiction of RowShifter (the two matrices shown) suggests a cyclic shift of columns within each block of p rows, but this is never explicitly stated. A reader cannot reproduce the operation without inferring the intended column-cyclic shift pattern. This ambiguity impairs reproducibility of the core algorithm.

comments.title: Underspecified filter matrix F in convolution Algorithm 2
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2 (line 13), the filter matrix F is declared as R^{m×n}. However, in the context of the convolution operation, variables m and n are not defined. The convolution uses image dimensions h and w, and the matrix used for the ciphertext ct.I is h×w. The use of m and n appears to be a carry-over from the matrix multiplication algorithm (where m is number of rows, n is number of columns). This is inconsistent: the convolution section defines its own dimensions (h, w, k), but the algorithm uses undefined symbols m, n. A reader cannot determine the correct dimensions of F, which is critical for the filtering step.

comments.title: Typo: 'matrixs' in Algorithm 1 output description
comments.quote: **Output:** The encrypted resulting matrixs ct _.C_ for _C ∈_ R _[m][×][p_]
comments.explanation: The word 'matrixs' is a typo (should be 'matrices' or 'matrix'). While minor, it appears in a formal algorithm specification. However, this does not affect mathematical correctness, so it is a formatting rather than technical issue. Not flagged further, but noted for completeness.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript bounds in τb definition for matrix B̄
comments.explanation: The encoding map τb is defined earlier in the paper (in the summary) as τb: B̄ = (b[1+(k%n)][1+((k/n)%p)])_{0≤k<m×n}. In the passage, after the equation involving the 3-column table, the same τb definition is repeated, but the subscript 1+((k/n)%p) uses p, whereas in the subsequent verbal description of the RowShifter operation, the text mentions 'b[1][1+(m−1)%p]' for the last element of the first column. The notation using p in the exponent of the modulo operation is inconsistent with the matrix size: B̄ is stated to be of size m×n, but the second index of b in τb ranges over p values (the number of columns of the original B). This is correct given that B has n rows and p columns, but the text immediately after the table shows entries with second indices like '1+(m−1)%p' for the last row, which is consistent. However, the definition's subscript '1+((k/n)%p)' uses p as the modulus, while the resulting matrix B̄ has n columns, not p columns. This creates confusion: the second index of b in the encoded matrix should run over p distinct kernel columns (since B has p columns), but the matrix B̄ is shown to have n columns (one per row of B). The mapping τb correctly takes the column index modulo p to repeat the p columns of B^T vertically to fill m rows, but the notation in the definition uses p, which is correct, but the reader might initially think the second coordinate should be modulo n because the matrix has n columns. The concern is resolved by context: the second index in τb refers to the column index of the original matrix B, which has p columns, not n. So no actual error, but the juxtaposition of p and n in close proximity without clarification could confuse a careful reader. The issue is minor because the table below clarifies the structure.

comments.title: Ambiguous specification of matrix F dimensions in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 7, matrix F is declared with dimensions R^{[m][×][n]}. However, the purpose of F is to filter out redundant values from the result of SumColVec, which operates on a matrix of size m×n. But later in the algorithm, the loop (lines 8-10) sets F[i][(i+idx)%n] = 1, which references a single column per row. This dimension choice (m×n) is consistent with the row-wise sum operation. However, the expected output C is of size m×p, and using an m×n filter matrix seems to produce m×p results only after accumulation across p iterations. This is correct but the comment could be misleading because F is used to extract one element per row from an m×n intermediate, not to produce the final p columns. The text does not explain why n is used instead of p, which is a nontrivial point. This underspecification makes it harder for the reader to verify the algorithm's correctness without working through the entire loop. Minor oversight.

comments.title: Mismatch between text and Algorithm 2: matrix F dimensions labeled R^{[m][×][n]} but context uses h,w
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, line 13, the comment states F ∈ R^{[m][×][n]}, but the algorithm is about convolution on an image of size h×w, and the loops (lines 14-20) iterate over hth from 0 to h-1 and wth from 0 to w-1. The dimensions m and n are not defined in the context of Algorithm 2's input (which uses h and w). The use of m and n here is inconsistent with the variable names used throughout the convolution section. This appears to be an OCR or author error: the algorithm was probably adapted from the matrix multiplication context (where m and n were defined) and the dimensions were not updated. The correct dimensions should be h×w, as the condition checks involve h and w. This is a moderate issue because it could lead a reader to misinterpret which variables are being used, and it conflicts with the local variable definitions.

comments.title: Missing specification of how SumForConv is implemented in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: ct.T ← SumForConv (ct.T)
comments.explanation: The paper introduces SumForConv as a new procedure in Section 2 (Preliminaries) and illustrates its result with a 4x4 example, but does not provide a formal definition or algorithm for how SumForConv is computed homomorphically. In Algorithm 2, SumForConv is called repeatedly (line 12) to aggregate blocks of size k×k. The reader is left to infer how this operation is implemented: whether it uses rotations, masking, or other HE primitives. Given that the algorithm is presented as a practical scheme, omitting the implementation of a central subroutine significantly hinders reproducibility. This is a major technical gap.

comments.title: Questionable claim that the impractical algorithm 'can be computed in parallel with k×k threads'
comments.quote: Note that Steps 1–3 in this algorithm can be computed in parallel with k × k threads.
comments.explanation: The text states that Steps 1–3 of the homomorphic convolution algorithm (Algorithm 2) can be computed in parallel with k×k threads. However, each iteration i (1 ≤ i ≤ k²) uses a different precomputed ciphertext ct.S[i] from Kernelspanner. While each iteration is independent (no data dependency between iterations), the claim that they can be parallelized with k×k threads is trivial and could overstate the practical benefit. In real HE implementations, memory bandwidth and the cost of generating the many kernel-spanned ciphertexts may dominate. The claim itself is logically true but could mislead a reader into thinking the overall latency reduces linearly, when the bottleneck is elsewhere. Minor overclaim.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a novel matrix-encoding method, termed "Volley Revolver," designed to enable privacy-preserving inference over encrypted data via homomorphic encryption. The manuscript establishes a clear theoretical framework and practical implementation for extending existing database encoding techniques to arbitrary matrix shapes, offering a distinct perspective on treating datasets as three-dimensional structures. While the work demonstrates good clarity in distinguishing itself from related approaches like Cryptonets and LoLa, the most significant technical concern lies in the proposed convolution strategy. Specifically, the method requires "spanning" or expanding convolution kernels into matrices of the same dimension as the input images, which introduces substantial computational redundancy and overhead. This approach must be rigorously evaluated against memory and time constraints, as it contradicts the efficiency goals central to homomorphic encryption research. Additionally, the reliance on the HEAAN (CKKS) library suggests potential constraints regarding cryptographic scheme generality; clarifying whether the proposed encoding is orthogonal to the underlying HE scheme or strictly dependent on CKKS parameters would strengthen the contribution.

comments.title: Operation Description-Name Contradiction for SumColVec
comments.quote: applies SumColVec on ct_.AB_[¯] 1 to collect the summation of the data in each row of AB_[¯] 1 ... 81. ... SumColVec , to compute the summation of each row and column respectively
comments.explanation: In Section 2 (Preliminaries), the text defines SumColVec as a procedure to compute the summation of each row (and column). In Section 3.1 (Technical Details), Step 2 describes SumColVec as a procedure to 'collect the summation of the data in each row'. While the description matches the intended action (summing rows), the standard nomenclature for an operation that sums across rows is SumRowVec, while SumColVec typically implies summing across columns. The inconsistency between the name 'SumColVec' and the textual description of row-wise summation (and the conflicting definition listing in Section 2) makes it extremely difficult for a reader to determine the correct function to implement or understand the algorithm's internal logic. The visual representation in Figure 1 suggests the operation outputs a vector of size equal to the number of rows, aligning with the description but contradicting the standard meaning of the name.

comments.title: Typographical Error in Citation Reference
comments.quote: Chanranne et al. [2] extended this work to deeper CNN using a different underlying software library called HElib [7]
comments.explanation: The name 'Chanranne' appears to be a typo in the citation. The seminal paper utilizing HElib for secure neural network inference (associated with the 'deeper CNN' claim following Cryptonets) is typically credited to 'Chen et al.' or similar authors in the HElib community (e.g., Chen, Shiwei, et al.). The name 'Chanranne' does not correspond to standard academic references in this domain.

comments.title: Corrupted Formula Text
comments.quote: where _s_ [ _i_ ][ _j_ ] =[] _[i] p_[+2] = _i_  _jq_ +2= _j[z]_[[] _[p]_[][] _[q]_[]][ for][ 1] _[ ≤][i, j][≤]_[2][.][In the convolutional layer,][ SumForConv][ can help]
comments.explanation: This line contains severely garbled text resulting from OCR errors, effectively making it unreadable. It appears to be an attempt to define a formula related to the 'SumForConv' procedure or convolution indices, but the symbols are mangled. The reader cannot discern the mathematical relationship or the definition being proposed, which is a critical gap for understanding the method's utility.

comments.title: Text-Diagram Mismatch in RowShifter
comments.quote: RowShifter pops up the first row of B¯ and appends another corresponding already existing row of B¯ :
comments.explanation: The textual description states that the operation 'pops up the first row', which implies a vertical (row-wise) shift. However, the accompanying visual diagram clearly shows a cyclic shift of columns (e.g., the elements of the first column are replaced by those of the second). A reader relying on the text would misunderstand the direction of the shift, which affects how the algorithm processes the columns of matrix B.

comments.title: Underspecified Generalization of SumForConv
comments.quote: Below we illustrate the result of SumForConv on Z taking the example that n and f are both 4 and the kernel size is 3 × 3: ... In the convolutional layer, SumForConv can help to compute some partial results of convolution operation for an image simultaneously.
comments.explanation: The procedure SumForConv is introduced only in the context of a specific, small example ($n=f=4$, kernel $3 	imes 3$). Later, in Algorithm 2, it is invoked generically to handle convolution with arbitrary kernel sizes and image dimensions ($h, w, k$). The paper fails to provide a generalized definition or explanation of how SumForConv adapts to these different sizes. It is unclear if SumForConv is a universal aggregation function or a specific fix for the example size.

comments.title: Overstated Assertion of Uselessness
comments.quote: However, its working-environment assumption... is too strict to make it a practical algorithm, leaving this algorithm directly useless.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the impractical algorithm, which requires an exact fit of image size to the ciphertext slots, is 'directly useless.' While the strict assumption makes the algorithm impractical for generic real-world use cases (e.g., arbitrary image resolutions), labeling it 'useless' is an overstatement. It could theoretically serve a purpose in debugging, theoretical analysis, or trivial scenarios where the input dimensions are known and match the assumption perfectly.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in convolution accumulator description
comments.quote: namely $ct_R = Add ( ct_R, ct_{ ar{D}} )$.
comments.explanation: The text describing Step 4 (lines 162-163) uses the variable name $ct_R$ to refer to the accumulator for the convolution operation. However, in the Algorithm 2 code block provided immediately following this text, the accumulator is clearly named $ct.Is$ (initialized at line 8). While the variable name $ct_R$ was established in the earlier matrix multiplication subsection (line 152) as an accumulator there, its reuse here contradicts the Algorithm 2 variable names and could confuse a reader checking the implementation details. (Note: The text also mentions $ct_{ ar{D}}$ as the accumulator result at this step, while the code simply uses $ct.T$ as a temporary variable; this discrepancy between the textual step description and code variable names is common, but the specific use of $ct_R$ for the accumulator is inconsistent with the algorithm's own definitions.)

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency in Checklist Regarding Theoretical Proofs
comments.quote: 2. (b) Did you include complete proofs of all theoretical results? [Yes]
comments.explanation: The provided paper summary explicitly states under 'Theorems & Propositions' that there are 'None'. If the authors are indeed not including theoretical results (i.e., lemmas or propositions proving the method's correctness), answering 'Yes' to having 'complete proofs' is factually incorrect. Even if they consider the derivation of equations as theoretical results, claiming 'complete proofs' when a section lists them as 'None' creates a contradiction between the text's explicit categorization and the meta-statement in the checklist.

comments.title: Citation Error in Reference [11]
comments.quote: [11] N.P. Smart and F. Vercauteren. Fully homomorphic simd operations. Cryptology ePrint Archive, Report 2011/133, 2011.
comments.explanation: This citation is problematic on multiple counts. N.P. Smart and F. Vercauteren are the primary authors of the BFV scheme ('Fully Homomorphic Encryption with Bounded Noise'), but the title 'Fully homomorphic simd operations' does not match their famous work, nor does it match the ePrint ID '2011/133'. The ID 2011/133 corresponds to 'Collision-free homomorphic cryptosystems' by Craig Gentry, Amit Sahai, and Brent Waters. The authors likely meant to cite the BFV paper (likely ePrint 2012/074 or an arXiv version) or a related work on SIMD operations, but the provided text presents an invalid combination of author, title, and archive ID.

comments.title: Bibliographic Formatting/Glitch in Reference [4]
comments.quote: [4] ... Faster cryp268 tonets ...
comments.explanation: The title of the reference 'Faster cryp268 tonets' appears to be corrupted, likely due to an OCR error. It likely intended to read 'Faster cryptoNets' or 'Faster CryptoNets', with 'cryptonets' split across the line and '268' being a spurious character (possibly a page number or a misread artifact). While this does not affect the technical content of the main paper, it renders the bibliography entry illegible and incorrect.
